metadata.notes: agent=yes (useMessageQueue, input.queueMessage); daemon=full (queue is in-memory React state, never written to transcript until streaming idle → contiguous working span, no flicker); driver=gap:keys (recipe needs the keys primitive to type a mid-turn follow-up; gemini-cli driver stubs keys, DRIVE_ELICITS lacks it)

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: gap:keys

## Reasoning

**agent = yes.** Gemini CLI 0.46.0 ships a first-class mid-turn message queue. The bundle defines `useMessageQueue` (packages/cli/src/ui/hooks/useMessageQueue.ts) with `addMessage`/`messageQueue` state and a keybinding `input.queueMessage`. While the agent is responding, a typed message submitted with Enter (or queued via the QUEUE_MESSAGE key) is appended to the queue rather than sent immediately. So the agent performs exactly the scenario's behavior — type a follow-up mid-turn, it is queued and consumed at a safe point.

**daemon = full.** The three spec observables (state stays `working` through the queued-message arrival; no `→ ready` flicker when the queued input lands; cycle still ends at `ready`) all hold for the gemini-cli adapter:

1. The queue lives entirely in React component state — `const [messageQueue2, setMessageQueue] = useState([])`; `addMessage` does `setMessageQueue(prev => [...prev, trimmedMessage])`. The queued text is NEVER passed to `submitQuery` (and therefore never written to the per-session JSONL transcript) until the flush effect fires, and that effect is gated on `streamingState === "idle"` — i.e. the current turn has fully completed. So during the working span the transcript receives no synthetic user-line for the queued text.
2. irrlicht's gemini-cli parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/geminicli/parser.go) is purely transcript-shape-driven. It settles to `ready` (`turn_done`) only on a `"gemini"` assistant message with non-empty final text and NO open tool calls; a `"user"` text message emits `user_message` (drives `working`). Since the queued text is absent from the transcript until consumption, the parser sees one contiguous `working` span and emits no premature `turn_done` → no `→ ready` flicker. It also never sees a `waiting` trigger from the queued input.
3. When the turn ends and `streamingState` goes idle, the queue is flushed: all queued messages are joined and submitted as a single new query, which appears in the transcript as a normal follow-up user turn that the parser drives `ready → working → ready` for. The cycle ends at `ready`. This is identical in shape to the claudecode reference cell.

**driver = gap:keys.** The recipe must type a second message into the live TUI while the agent is mid-turn — without the driver's normal `send`→`wait_turn` cycle (which would block until the first turn finishes, defeating the queueing). That is the `keys` primitive (raw tmux key sequence). The gemini-cli interactive driver (replaydata/agents/gemini-cli/driver-interactive.sh) stubs the `keys` arm as `not_implemented`, and its `DRIVE_ELICITS` constant is `send slash wait_turn sleep` — `keys` is absent, so recipe-lint flags this recipe as a semantic_gap before recording. `record` must port the `keys` seam (tmux `send-keys -l` of the text + Enter, fired during the working span) from the claudecode reference driver, then drive.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's OK)

The queue itself is invisible to irrlicht — there is no event for 'user typed text now buffered in React state'. The spec asserts no such event; it asserts only state invariants on the working span, all of which the daemon verifies from its event stream.

## Provisional note

The `daemon=full` verdict is grounded in the parser code plus the agent's queue-flush source (queued text only reaches the transcript at streaming-idle). It is provisional on emission ordering — that Gemini writes nothing transcript-side for the queued text mid-turn — which a live recording (after the driver `keys` gap is ported) will promote from provisional to settled. Hence confidence 0.8, not higher.
caveats: Gemini queues a mid-turn message when the user submits with Enter while the agent is responding (addMessage → messageQueue state). The driver's keys step should type the follow-up text and press Enter once during the working span; the QUEUE_MESSAGE keybinding (Tab) is an alternative but the Enter-while-responding path is the canonical queue.

The flush effect joins all queued messages with \n\n and submits them as ONE query at streamingState idle — so multiple mid-turn lines collapse into a single follow-up turn. The spec accommodates this: it asserts the working span stays contiguous and the cycle ends at ready, not the consumption shape.

If a future Gemini CLI ever persisted queued text to the transcript as a synthetic user-line BEFORE consumption, the parser would emit user_message (and the turn-shape could flip toward ready). That regression is exactly what this scenario's no-flicker invariant catches — which is the test's purpose.
driver=gap:keys is tooling, not an observability limit: the cell is a real cell with a real recipe; record ports the keys seam from drive-claudecode-interactive.sh before driving.

sources.note: useMessageQueue hook (v0.46.0 bundle): messageQueue lives in React useState; addMessage appends; flush effect submits the joined queue to submitQuery ONLY when streamingState === 'idle' — queued text never hits the transcript mid-turn. Also defines input.queueMessage keybinding (Tab).

Transcript-shape-driven state: turn_done (settle to ready) only on a 'gemini' message with non-empty text and no tool calls; 'user' text → user_message (working). With queued text absent from the transcript until consumption, the parser sees one contiguous working span and no premature settle.
keys arm is not_implemented; DRIVE_ELICITS='send slash wait_turn sleep' (no keys) → recipe-lint semantic_gap for a recipe needing keys. record must port the keys seam before driving.

Reference cell for this scenario: same daemon shape (queued text buffered inside the agent, not written to transcript until consumed → contiguous working span); recipe uses a keys step for the mid-turn follow-up.

preconditions: gemini CLI >=0.46.0 on PATH and authenticated via Google OAuth (set up out-of-band; Gemini CLI stops serving Google One / unpaid tiers 2026-06-18)
irrlichd is recording (dev daemon with --record; isolated IRRLICHT_HOME + alt port to coexist with prod)
Gemini launched with -y (yolo) so neither prompt trips a tool-permission dialog
DRIVER: the keys step type must be ported into drive-gemini-cli-interactive.sh (tmux send-keys -l of the text + Enter, fired during the working span) and added to DRIVE_ELICITS before recording
setup: Driver launches gemini -y under tmux in a fresh trusted cwd; Gemini mints its own sessionId (harvested from the transcript header)
The first prompt must keep gemini busy long enough (a few seconds) to type a follow-up into the TUI — a slow count-to-10 with explicit pauses is sufficient

The mid-turn follow-up is delivered via the keys step (tmux send-keys ending in Enter) while streamingState is responding; Gemini's useMessageQueue buffers it in React state and flushes it to submitQuery only when the turn reaches idle

script.text: Count from 1 to 10, one number per line, with a brief pause between each. Then on the last line reply with exactly the word: done

script.keys: f o l l o w u p Space q u e u e d Enter

verify: events.jsonl shows the session entering ready, then a ready→working transition for the first prompt
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between the first ready→working and the queued-message consumption (the working span is contiguous through the queued arrival)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span
events.jsonl eventually shows a working→ready transition; final session state is ready
transcript shows the initial user prompt; the queued follow-up appears only as a later user line (consumed as a new turn at streaming idle) — never as a synthetic user-line during the working span
